title: Composition over inheritance

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Composition over inheritance; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Interfaces, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and composition guidance; atomic topic: Composition over inheritance
sources.claim: Composition gives an object owned or injected collaborators and delegates focused work, allowing reuse without claiming a subtype relationship.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Composition gives an object owned or injected collaborators and delegates focused work, allowing reuse without claiming a subtype relationship.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A ReportService receives a Formatter interface and delegates formatting, so output policy changes independently of report identity. Your goal is to refactor a fragile hierarchy into composed collaborators with testable interfaces. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; Overriding and dynamic dispatch. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Composition gives an object owned or injected collaborators and delegates focused work, allowing reuse without claiming a subtype relationship. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Composition still needs clear ownership and lifecycle, excessive forwarding can obscure an interface, circular dependencies create coupling, and shared mutable collaborators need coordination. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Composition means copying another class's methods into a wrapper and exposing all of them unchanged.

includes: Delegate to owned collaborators when reuse does not require subtype substitution.
Refactor a fragile hierarchy into composed collaborators with testable interfaces.
excludes: Composition still needs clear ownership and lifecycle, excessive forwarding can obscure an interface, circular dependencies create coupling, and shared mutable collaborators need coordination.

outcomes: Refactor a fragile hierarchy into composed collaborators with testable interfaces.

examples.title: Composition over inheritance: direct language case
examples.situation: A ReportService receives a Formatter interface and delegates formatting, so output policy changes independently of report identity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A ReportService receives a Formatter interface and delegates formatting, so output policy changes independently of report identity.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Compose a collaborator behind a purpose-built contract and delegate only behavior that preserves the owning object's invariants.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Compose a collaborator behind a purpose-built contract and delegate only behavior that preserves the owning object's invariants.

examples.title: Composition over inheritance: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A Stack wraps an ArrayDeque instead of extending it, preventing callers from using queue operations that violate the stack contract.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A Stack wraps an ArrayDeque instead of extending it, preventing callers from using queue operations that violate the stack contract.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Composition still needs clear ownership and lifecycle, excessive forwarding can obscure an interface, circular dependencies create coupling, and shared mutable collaborators need coordination.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Composition means copying another class's methods into a wrapper and exposing all of them unchanged.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Refactor a fragile hierarchy into composed collaborators with testable interfaces.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Composition means copying another class's methods into a wrapper and exposing all of them unchanged.
misconceptions.correction: Compose a collaborator behind a purpose-built contract and delegate only behavior that preserves the owning object's invariants.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Composition means copying another class's methods into a wrapper and exposing all of them unchanged.

analogy.example: a plug-and-socket system in which contracts describe compatible behavior while components hide their implementation can introduce Composition over inheritance, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: overload resolution, method hiding, constructor order, and multiple interface defaults require Java-specific rules
Composition still needs clear ownership and lifecycle, excessive forwarding can obscure an interface, circular dependencies create coupling, and shared mutable collaborators need coordination.

recap.summary: You should now be able to refactor a fragile hierarchy into composed collaborators with testable interfaces. Keep this boundary in view: Composition still needs clear ownership and lifecycle, excessive forwarding can obscure an interface, circular dependencies create coupling, and shared mutable collaborators need coordination.
